questions.text: In an economy that relies upon barter,

options.text: trade does not require a double coincidence of wants.
scarce resources are allocated just as easily as they are in economies that do not rely upon barter.
there is no item in the economy that is widely accepted in exchange for goods and services.
All of the above are correct.

questions.text: Economists use the word "money" to refer to

options.text: income generated by the production of goods and services.
those assets regularly used to buy goods and services.
the value of a person's assets.
the value of stocks and bonds.

questions.text: Which type of money has intrinsic value?

options.text: commodity money
fiat money
both commodity money and fiat money
neither commodity money nor fiat money

questions.text: The Federal Reserve does all except which of the following?

options.text: It controls the supply of money.
It acts as a lender of last resort to banks.
It makes loans to large business firms.
It tries to ensure the health of the banking system.

questions.text: Which of the following does the Federal Reserve not do?

options.text: conduct monetary policy
act as a lender of last resort
convert Federal Reserve Notes into gold
serve as a bank regulator
short-run tradeoff between inflation and unemployment.
short-run tradeoff between an increase in the money supply and inflation.
long-run tradeoff between inflation and unemployment.
long-run tradeoff between an increase in the money supply and inflation.

questions.text: Suppose that banks desire to hold no excess reserves, the reserve requirement is 5 percent, and a bank receives a new deposit of $1,000. This bank

options.text: will increase its required reserves by $50.
will initially see its total reserves increase by $1,000.
will be able to make a new loan of $950.
All of the above are correct.

questions.text: Suppose banks desire to hold no excess reserves. If the reserve requirement is 15 percent and if a bank receives a new deposit of $10, then this bank

options.text: must increase its required reserves by $10.
will initially see its total reserves increase by $15.
will be able to make new loans up to a maximum of $8.50.
All of the above are correct.

questions.text: If banks desire to hold no excess reserves, the reserve ratio is 10 percent, and a bank that was previously just meeting its reserve requirement receives a new deposit of $400, then initially the bank has a

options.text: $400 increase in excess reserves and no increase in required reserves.
$400 increase in required reserves and no increase in excess reserves.
$360 increase in excess reserves and $40 increase in required reserves.
$40 increase in excess reserves and $360 increase in required reserves.

questions.text: If the reserve ratio is 12.5 percent, the money multiplier is

options.text: 25. Scenario 29-2. The Monetary Policy of Tazi is controlled by the country’s central bank known as the Bank of Tazi. The local unit of currency is the Taz. Aggregate banking statistics show that collectively the banks of Tazi hold 300 million Tazes of required reserves, 75 million Tazes of excess reserves, have issued 7,500 million Tazes of deposits, and hold 225 million Tazes of Tazian Treasury bonds. Tazians prefer to use only demand deposits and so all money is on deposit at the bank.

questions.text: Refer to Scenario 29-2. Suppose that the Bank of Tazi changes the reserve requirement ratio to 3 percent. Assuming that the banks still want to hold the same percentage of excess reserves what is the value of the money supply after the change in the reserve requirement ratio?

options.text: 9,375 million Tazes
10,000 million Tazes
12,500 million Tazes
None of the above is correct to the nearest million salidos.

questions.text: Which of the following lists two things that both increase the money supply?

options.text: lower the discount rate, raise the reserve requirement
lower the discount rate, lower the reserve requirement
raise the discount rate, raise the reserve requirement
raise the discount rate, lower the reserve requirement

questions.text: Which of the following lists two things that both decrease the money supply?

options.text: make open market purchases, raise the reserve requirement
make open market purchases, lower the reserve requirement
make open market sales, raise the reserve requirement
make open market sales, lower the reserve requirement

questions.text: Suppose banks decide to hold more excess reserves relative to deposits. Other things the same, this action will cause the

options.text: money supply to fall. To reduce the impact of this the Fed could lower the discount rate.
money supply to fall. To reduce the impact of this the Fed could raise the discount rate.
money supply to rise. To reduce the impact of this the Fed could lower the discount rate.
money supply to rise. To reduce the impact of this the Fed could raise the discount rate.

questions.text: When the Fed conducts open-market sales,

options.text: it sells Treasury securities, which increases the money supply.
it sells Treasury securities, which decreases the money supply.
it borrows from member banks, which increases the money supply.
it lends money to member banks, which decreases the money supply.

questions.text: The Fed’s primary tool to change the money supply is

options.text: changing the discount rate.
changing the reserve requirement.
conducting open market operations.
redeeming Federal Reserve notes.
